items.title: Quality management principles
items.description: Quality management is the disciplined practice of consistently meeting customer and stakeholder requirements while continually improving how work is performed. Its guiding principles typically include customer focus, engaged leadership, involvement of people, a process approach, evidence-based decision making, relationship management, and a commitment to continual improvement. Together these principles shift an organization from reactive firefighting toward predictable, measurable outcomes governed by documented processes and feedback loops.
items.applicability: Applies to any team or organization that wants repeatable delivery quality, and to control owners establishing a quality management system or aligning engineering practices with one.
items.example: A delivery team defines its release process as a documented workflow with entry/exit criteria, measures defect escape rate per release, reviews the metric monthly, and feeds findings back into the next sprint's process adjustments.
items.limitation: This is a practical control-style summary in original wording; it does not replace the licensed standard or formal legal/compliance advice, and the platform makes no claim of certification.

items.title: Information security principles (confidentiality, integrity, availability)
items.description: Information security is commonly framed around three core properties: confidentiality (information is disclosed only to those authorized to see it), integrity (information and processing remain accurate, complete, and unaltered except through authorized changes), and availability (information and services are accessible when legitimately needed). Effective programs balance these three properties against business risk rather than maximizing any single one in isolation. Controls are selected, implemented, and measured to preserve these properties across people, processes, and technology throughout the information lifecycle.
items.applicability: Applies to any system that stores, processes, or transmits sensitive data, and to control owners classifying assets and selecting protective controls.
items.example: A banking middleware service encrypts customer data at rest and in transit (confidentiality), validates and checksums message payloads to detect tampering (integrity), and runs redundant instances behind a load balancer with a recovery time objective (availability).
items.limitation: This is a practical control-style summary in original wording; it does not replace the licensed standard or formal legal/compliance advice, and the platform makes no claim of certification.

items.title: Access control objective
items.description: The objective of access control is to ensure that only authenticated and authorized identities can reach information and system functions, and only to the extent their role legitimately requires. It rests on least privilege (grant the minimum access needed), need-to-know, segregation of incompatible duties, and timely revocation when access is no longer required. Access decisions should be enforced consistently, logged, and periodically reviewed so that entitlements do not accumulate beyond what each role warrants.
items.applicability: Applies to any application, database, or infrastructure component with multiple users or roles, and to control owners defining roles, entitlements, and recertification cycles.
items.example: A platform enforces role-based access so analysts can read project knowledge but only approvers can promote items to the approved set; access grants are reviewed quarterly and departing staff are deprovisioned within one business day.
items.limitation: This is a practical control-style summary in original wording; it does not replace the licensed standard or formal legal/compliance advice, and the platform makes no claim of certification.

items.title: Auditability and evidence
items.description: Auditability is the property that lets an independent reviewer reconstruct what happened, when, by whom, and under what authorization, using reliable records produced by the system. It depends on capturing complete, accurate, and tamper-resistant evidence such as logs, approvals, and change records, retained for a defined period and protected from unauthorized modification. Strong auditability turns assertions about how controls operate into verifiable facts, which is the basis of trust in regulated environments.
items.applicability: Applies to any control or system whose operation may need to be demonstrated to auditors, regulators, or management, and to control owners designing logging and retention.
items.example: Every approval action in the knowledge lifecycle writes an append-only audit entry recording the actor, timestamp, item, and decision, so an internal auditor can later trace exactly who approved a business rule and when.
items.limitation: This is a practical control-style summary in original wording; it does not replace the licensed standard or formal legal/compliance advice, and the platform makes no claim of certification.

items.title: Document and version control
items.description: Document and version control ensures that the right version of a controlled document is identifiable, available where needed, and protected from unintended change. It covers unique identification, review and approval before issue, indication of current status, distribution control, and the orderly handling of superseded or obsolete versions so they are not used by mistake. The goal is that everyone works from a single authoritative source and that the history of changes remains reconstructable.
items.applicability: Applies to policies, procedures, specifications, and controlled records, and to control owners managing a documented management system or engineering documentation set.
items.example: A policy carries a version number, an owner, an effective date, and a change log; the previous version is archived and clearly marked superseded, and staff reference only the current published copy from the controlled repository.
items.limitation: This is a practical control-style summary in original wording; it does not replace the licensed standard or formal legal/compliance advice, and the platform makes no claim of certification.

items.title: Incident handling lifecycle
items.description: Incident handling is the structured lifecycle for detecting, responding to, and learning from events that disrupt operations or threaten security. A typical lifecycle moves through preparation, detection and reporting, assessment and triage by severity, containment, eradication and recovery, and a post-incident review that captures lessons and corrective actions. Defined roles, communication paths, and timelines keep response consistent under pressure and feed improvements back into prevention.
items.applicability: Applies to security events, outages, and data-handling failures, and to control owners establishing response procedures, severity criteria, and escalation paths.
items.example: On detecting unauthorized access attempts, the on-call engineer classifies the event as high severity, isolates the affected service, restores from a known-good state, then holds a blameless review that produces tracked remediation tasks.
items.limitation: This is a practical control-style summary in original wording; it does not replace the licensed standard or formal legal/compliance advice, and the platform makes no claim of certification.

items.title: Change management control
items.description: Change management controls how modifications to systems, configurations, and processes are proposed, assessed, approved, implemented, and reviewed so that change does not introduce uncontrolled risk. Core elements include a documented request, impact and risk assessment, appropriate authorization, testing, a rollback plan, and post-implementation verification. Separating who requests, who approves, and who implements changes reinforces accountability and reduces the chance of error or abuse.
items.applicability: Applies to production systems, infrastructure, and controlled configurations, and to control owners defining change approval workflows and emergency-change handling.
items.example: A database schema change is raised as a request with an impact assessment and rollback script, approved by a change authority, applied through an automated pipeline in a maintenance window, and verified before the change is closed.
items.limitation: This is a practical control-style summary in original wording; it does not replace the licensed standard or formal legal/compliance advice, and the platform makes no claim of certification.

items.title: Risk-based thinking
items.description: Risk-based thinking is the practice of identifying what could go wrong, judging likelihood and impact, and directing effort and controls toward the risks that matter most rather than treating all issues equally. It is proactive and continuous: risks and opportunities are assessed during planning, addressed through proportionate actions, and revisited as conditions change. This approach lets an organization prioritize scarce resources, justify control decisions, and avoid both under-protection and wasteful over-engineering.
items.applicability: Applies to planning, design, and operational decisions across any process, and to control owners deciding where to invest controls and what residual risk to accept.
items.example: Before importing a legacy banking project, the team rates the risk of mishandling sensitive code as high-impact, so it applies stronger access controls and audit logging there while accepting lighter controls on low-sensitivity documentation.
items.limitation: This is a practical control-style summary in original wording; it does not replace the licensed standard or formal legal/compliance advice, and the platform makes no claim of certification.

items.title: Business continuity basics
items.description: Business continuity is the capability to keep critical activities running, or to resume them within acceptable timeframes, despite disruption. It starts with identifying critical processes and their dependencies, setting recovery objectives such as how quickly a service must return (recovery time) and how much data loss is tolerable (recovery point), and then building and testing plans to meet them. Continuity arrangements are only credible when they are exercised regularly and kept current as the business changes.
items.applicability: Applies to services whose interruption would materially harm the organization, and to control owners defining recovery objectives, plans, and test schedules.
items.example: A team identifies its core middleware as critical, sets a four-hour recovery time objective and a fifteen-minute recovery point objective, maintains tested backups and a failover runbook, and rehearses recovery twice a year.
items.limitation: This is a practical control-style summary in original wording; it does not replace the licensed standard or formal legal/compliance advice, and the platform makes no claim of certification.

items.title: Control ownership
items.description: Control ownership assigns a named, accountable person to each control who is responsible for ensuring it is designed appropriately, operates as intended, and is evidenced and improved over time. Clear ownership prevents controls from drifting into neglect, clarifies who answers for failures, and gives auditors a single point of contact. Owners are expected to understand the control's objective, monitor its effectiveness, and escalate when it cannot be sustained.
items.applicability: Applies to every documented control in a management system or risk framework, and to control owners and managers maintaining a control register with assigned accountability.
items.example: The access-recertification control is owned by the platform security lead, who confirms each quarter that reviews ran, retains the sign-off as evidence, and escalates to management if a review is missed.
items.limitation: This is a practical control-style summary in original wording; it does not replace the licensed standard or formal legal/compliance advice, and the platform makes no claim of certification.

items.title: Evidence collection
items.description: Evidence collection is the deliberate capture and retention of records that demonstrate a control was performed and operated effectively over a period, not just at a single moment. Good evidence is relevant, sufficient, reliable, attributable to a person or system, time-stamped, and protected against later alteration. Collecting it as a routine byproduct of the control, rather than reconstructing it before an audit, keeps it trustworthy and reduces last-minute effort.
items.applicability: Applies to any control that must be demonstrated to auditors or management, and to control owners defining what artifacts prove operation and how long to keep them.
items.example: For monthly backup verification, the owner retains the automated test report, the restore log, and a dated sign-off, so an auditor sampling any month finds contemporaneous proof the control ran.
items.limitation: This is a practical control-style summary in original wording; it does not replace the licensed standard or formal legal/compliance advice, and the platform makes no claim of certification.

items.title: Internal audit readiness
items.description: Internal audit readiness is the ongoing state in which an organization can demonstrate its controls operate effectively at any time, without a scramble of preparation. It rests on a current control register, identified owners, routinely collected evidence, tracked corrective actions from prior findings, and a clear mapping between requirements and the controls that satisfy them. Readiness is maintained by periodic self-assessment and management review so that gaps are found and closed before an external party finds them.
items.applicability: Applies to teams subject to internal or external audit, and to control owners and audit liaisons preparing for assurance reviews and tracking remediation.
items.example: Ahead of an internal audit, the control owner produces a register mapping each requirement to its control, sample evidence for the period, and a status report on remediation of the previous cycle's findings, so the audit confirms rather than discovers the control state.
items.limitation: This is a practical control-style summary in original wording; it does not replace the licensed standard or formal legal/compliance advice, and the platform makes no claim of certification.
